title: Paris-Brest
subtitle: French choux pastry ring filled with hazelnut praline cream
excerpt: A large choux pastry ring piped and baked whole, split horizontally, and filled with a hazelnut praline cream. The pastry ring was created in 1910 to celebrate a Paris to Brest bicycle race; its wheel shape references the bicycle tyre. The choux must be fully dried in the oven to prevent collapse when cut. The praline cream is made from a hazelnut praline blended into whipped cream.
sourceNotes: Paris-Brest was created in 1910 by pastry chef Louis Durand of Maisons-Laffitte, commissioned to celebrate the Paris-Brest-Paris cycling race. The recipe entered French patisserie literature widely by the 1920s. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903 first edition, Project Gutenberg), gives the foundational choux pastry method (pâte à choux) and mousseline cream on which Paris-Brest is built, though the assembled Paris-Brest post-dates that edition. The combination of choux, praline, and mousseline is documented in Henri-Paul Pellaprat, L'Art Culinaire Moderne (1936).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the choux ring up to 8 hours ahead and store at room temperature uncovered. Make the praline ahead and keep at room temperature. Fill and assemble no more than 2 hours before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 180°C. Bake for 30 minutes until well risen and deep golden, then reduce to 170°C conventional (150°C fan) and bake for a further 15 to 20 minutes until very crisp all over. Do not open the oven during the first 25 minutes.
baking.decoratingTechnique: piped praline cream, flaked almond topping, icing sugar

recipeTools.notes: large baking tray, lined with baking paper
large star tip for choux ring, medium star tip for cream filling

glossaryTerms.definition: The first stage of making choux pastry, in which butter, water (and sometimes milk), sugar, and salt are brought to a full boil, then all the flour is added at once and the mixture is beaten vigorously over the heat for 1 to 2 minutes to form a smooth, ball-shaped dough that pulls cleanly away from the sides of the pan. This drying-out step (the panade) cooks off excess moisture and ensures the starch gelatinises fully, which gives the choux enough structure to hold its piped shape. If the panade is not dry enough, the finished choux will be dense and flat. The correct endpoint is a light film of dough visible on the base of the pan.

content.text: : the flour is cooked into the boiling butter and water until a smooth ball forms. The eggs are beaten in after the dough cools slightly. The ring must bake until completely dry inside to prevent collapse when split; the two-temperature bake achieves this. The praline filling can be as simple as crushed toasted hazelnuts stirred into whipped cream.

attrs.prepNote: for sprinkling on top before baking
skin-on, toasted and skins rubbed off

content.text: Choux: heat the oven to 200°C conventional (180°C fan). Draw a 20cm circle on baking paper as a guide, flip it pencil-side down on the tray. Bring the water, milk, butter, salt, and sugar to a full boil. Add all the flour at once and beat vigorously over medium heat for 1 to 2 minutes until the dough pulls cleanly from the pan sides and a light film coats the base. Tip into the mixer bowl and beat on medium speed for 1 minute to cool slightly. Add the beaten eggs gradually, beating well between each addition, until the dough is smooth and glossy and falls in a thick ribbon. Load into a piping bag with a large star tip and pipe two overlapping rings around the traced circle to form a tyre shape. Scatter flaked almonds over the top. Bake for 30 minutes, reduce to 170°C conventional (150°C fan), and bake for 15 to 20 more minutes until very crisp and deep golden all over. Cool completely on a rack.

Praline: melt the sugar in a dry pan over medium heat, swirling (not stirring) until a deep amber caramel forms. Add the toasted hazelnuts, toss to coat, and pour onto a baking paper-lined tray. Leave to harden. Break into pieces and blitz in a food processor to a coarse or smooth paste as preferred. Whip the cream and icing sugar to firm peaks, then fold in the praline paste.

Assembly: split the choux ring horizontally with a serrated knife. Remove any uncooked dough from the inside. Fill the bottom half generously with praline cream, piping in rosettes or spreading with a spoon. Replace the top and dust with icing sugar. Serve within 2 hours.
